22.1.label: Question 22.1
22.1.question: Outline some of the problems an insurer may face if providing free health checks on its products.

Problems with free health checks: the cost of providing the health checks needs to be met - premiums will either need to be increased or profitability will reduce
claim incidence may increase as a result of the health checks identifying more illnesses and impairments
customers in the target market may not require the health checks or may not consider them to be of value - this may result in lower sales volumes than expected
alternatively, it may encourage lapses (on the insurer's existing products) and re-entry (on the products with health checks)
a service level agreement will need to be established and maintained with a health check provider, and administration systems put in place, increasing costs
the insurer will need to decide how simple or comprehensive the check will be and how frequently it will be offered (eg every two years)

22.2.label: Question 22.2
22.2.scenario:
A company has been a leading provider of private medical insurance for many years. The company's most successful individual product is a policy that restricts the insured's care to a list of approved hospitals.

Recent market research conducted on behalf of the company suggests that consumers are becoming concerned as much with the quality of the care they receive whilst in hospital as with the level of the premiums they pay.

Although each hospital needs to meet 'industry-recognised' quality standards to be on the approved list, this concern over quality has also been expressed by several of the company's own policyholders.

The directors of the company are considering how the design and marketing of its policies might be changed to address this quality concern.
22.2.question: Explain how this situation may have arisen and suggest how the company might address this concern.

Reasons why existing customers are conce: Quality standards simply may not have been high enough to satisfy those policyholders who have expressed their concerns.
The results of the quality assessments may be out of date, so that standards may have fallen in certain hospitals that met the criteria when they were last reviewed.
It may be the result of random fluctuations. A few policyholders have been dissatisfied, but the majority may have been happy with the care in the hospitals.
However, perhaps the most likely explanation is that the measure of quality from the consumers' viewpoint is different to that used in the healthcare industry.
Those funding healthcare, for example, will be concerned with the efficient running of the hospital and the efficient treatment of patients.
For the most part they will be concerned with costs rather than the quality of treatment.
For example, a hospital might be judged on the: number of bed days it offers each year; number of patients it treats per available bed day; average cost of each type of treatment or operation.
Patients, however, may be more concerned with: the non-recurrence of symptoms following treatment; the average length of stay in hospital; the comfort of the hospital stay.
Although there will be some overlap, any statistics published by a hospital monitoring organisation are unlikely to be focused solely on patient concerns.
In addition, some data may only be available on a regional or national basis and so it will not be possible to identify hospitals from the approved list.
Ensuring quality: The reasons for the concerns of the existing policyholders should be identified and addressed.
Quality standards should be reviewed to ensure they are up to date and relevant for policyholders.

Measures could be imposed as part of the insurer's contract with an approved hospital. Ideally, the criteria for approval should be set by the insurer, ... but with the requirement for quality of care from the individual patient's point of view kept firmly in mind.
For example, contractual standards could be imposed on: size of rooms; standard of decoration; facilities provided; level of nursing cover; standards of cleanliness; standards of catering.

A further standard could be based on a measure of patient satisfaction of the company's policyholders. This could be from the results of a short questionnaire given to patients about that hospital stay. Each hospital might need to meet a certain level of satisfaction to remain on the list.
They should still also include standards related to the quality of medical care, such as non-recurrence rates.
However, the insurer will also need to bear in mind the need to control costs, and so keep premiums competitive. Therefore, standards to do with cost efficiency may also be required.
Hospitals could be asked to provide regular monitoring data as part of the process of contract renewal.
This information could be supplemented by the results of periodical inspections of hospitals (although this might be expensive).
All these measures would increase overhead costs, but this should be offset by increased volumes of sales and increased persistency.
The greater opportunity to control treatment costs might also help reduce claims costs.
The company needs to be careful because if it formally approves a hospital, it might become legally responsible for the quality of care in it, and so the legal position needs to be checked.
Policy design suggestions: Offer a restricted range of hospitals, so that insured patients have less choice, but at a higher level of quality.
Could offer several hospital lists, so that policyholders can have a greater choice of hospitals, ... but can choose the level of comfort they want, for an increased premium.
Include extra benefits aimed at enhancing the quality of the patient's experience - for example transport to and from hospital, or flowers to welcome a patient home.
These benefits should have a high perceived value to the customer for relatively low cost.
Marketing suggestions: the company should place greater emphasis in its marketing on the quality of care, to reflect consumer opinion.
Once the concern around quality of care in the approved hospitals has been addressed, the company can promote and describe these quality standards specifically in its literature.
Statistical data from feedback on quality from customers, such as results of the patient satisfaction questionnaires mentioned above, could be used in marketing.
The responses from the questionnaire could be used to generate case studies and similar marketing stories. The insurer would need to address data protection issues in these cases.
Give greater emphasis to quality in the renewal documentation, so as to encourage persistency.

22.3.label: Question 22.3
22.3.scenario:
A health and care insurance company, based in a developed country, has been one of the market leaders in the critical illness (CI) insurance market for several years. Most business is sold through financial advisers.

Results of research into the domestic protection insurance market have indicated that, in recent years, term assurance premiums have fallen, while the premiums for stand-alone CI policies have risen.
22.3.question: N/A

(i).question: Describe the factors that could have led to this situation.

Factors leading to recent changes in pre: As this is a developed country, it is likely that mortality experience has been improving at all ages.
This will have led to the use of lighter mortality rates in premium bases and hence lower term assurance premiums.
Sales of term assurance may be strong, as it is often a prerequisite of purchasing a mortgage.
High sales volumes allow insurers to spread overheads and thus keep premiums low.
The main driver for the increase in CI insurance premiums may have been increases in reinsurance rates on this business.
CI reinsurance is normally on a risk premium basis, and so the effect of this on insurers would be immediate.
Reasons for an increase in insurer / reinsurer rates:
Rates may have increased due to a deterioration in overall CI experience.

The increased incidence may be as a result of changing medical technology, making certain operations more readily available, and enabling the diagnosis of certain conditions possible at an earlier stage, both of which may have increased claim incidence rates.
The effect of improving mortality rates has been to increase expected CI claims, and hence premiums.
In particular, for conditions such as cancer and heart attacks, survival rates shortly after incidence have greatly improved.
This would have increased the numbers of people living beyond the survival period on many CI policies.
CI claim incidence rates for specific illnesses may also have increased, eg liver disease.

Strong competition in the CI market may have forced insurers to cut premiums in order to capture market share, ... but a shift in the underwriting cycle may now have led rates to increase as insurers exit the market.
Insurers may be increasingly recognising the uncertainty around the expected future experience.

In particular: the potential impact of screening programmes on future claims; the potential impact of misrepresentation on future claims; the extent of cases that may have occurred but are still unreported.
These may have led to increased reserves and solvency capital, and hence premiums.
Hence, greater margins for this uncertainty could be being allowed for in premium bases, particularly on policies with guaranteed premiums and terms.

Reviewable policies may also require higher margins, because there are restrictions on the extent that premiums or terms can be changed if required, eg due to competitive pressures and the requirement to treat customers fairly.
Some insurers may have added additional benefits to their policies (such as health checks) and increased premiums as a result.
Increased expenses, required to comply with new regulations, may have affected premiums.

(ii).question: Discuss the merits of these suggestions in the light of the company's objectives: (a) restricted cover (b) temporary annuity benefit (c) benefits proportional based on illness (d) use of direct marketing.

(a) Restricted cover: The company will need to decide which medical conditions should be covered. These should be those that are perceived to be the most commonly occurring or the most feared by customers.
One problem is that the most frequently occurring conditions account for the majority of CI claims.
Therefore, if all the 'major risks' are to be covered, this will not reduce the number of claims by much and so the reduction in premium levels may not be sufficient to achieve the company's aims.
If cover is restricted further (eg to include only stroke, heart attack and certain cancers), the policy may not be seen as being adequate, and so not attractive.
A balance needs to be sought between cost and coverage. Market research together with a breakdown of expected costs by condition will be useful to assess this.
It would be wise not to have a 'catch all' category such as a total permanent disablement benefit. Otherwise, cases that the policy is designed to exclude may still be covered.
Customers will need to be made clear at outset that this policy only pays out on certain conditions, and not on any serious or critical illness.
Additional sales training and clearly-worded policy literature will be required.
Otherwise there is a high risk of disputed claims and ombudsman decisions against the company.
There may be moral pressure on the company to pay claims on rare, but severely debilitating, conditions that the policyholder expected to be covered.
With a limited number of conditions for payment, underwriting may be simpler and more accurate.

Assuming the company has claims data broken down by each condition, pricing may be more accurate, because the conditions excluded are likely to be those where there is less data, and hence more uncertainty as regards future experience. Hence contingency margins could be lower.
(b) Benefits as a temporary life annuity: Customers may perceive this to be value for money if they compare premiums for the total benefit (ie 60 monthly payments) with that for the equivalent lump sum CI policy. In which case, the new policy will be cheaper.

However, a regular income may be less appealing to customers who may be attracted by high amounts ('£60,000 cash' looks much better than '£1,000 per month income for up to five years'), and the immediacy and flexibility of cash lump sums.
Customers looking for replacement income (eg if unable to work) in the event of serious illness at a low cost may be attracted to this policy.

However, the cover is much more restrictive than that available on income protection insurance because only the insured conditions are covered, so the customer may be unable to work and not be covered. In addition, payments stop after five years.
This form of benefit is less simple than a lump sum and this might be a barrier to sales.

Casing benefits after five years may cause disappointment and problems for some claimants. For example they may have become dependent on the income to provide nursing care. These may result in ill-will towards the company.
Policyholders would need to be made clearly aware, and regularly reminded, that the benefit would stop after five years.

The effect of tax should be investigated and whether this change affects the tax that would be payable on the benefit. Any worsening of the tax position is likely to make the product less appealing to consumers.
The length of the deferred period is important. The length of the survival period on the existing policy would be a good starting point in deciding upon this.
A long deferred period would reduce claims costs further and provides an opportunity for the validation of a claim.
However, an extended deferred period is unlikely to match the expectations of and the risks faced by the policyholder (eg requiring the finance to pay for medical treatment).

Unless the benefit is set to vary according to the type of illness (see next section), a policyholder with a more serious and life-threatening condition is likely to receive a smaller benefit in total than one with an insured but less serious condition that is not life-threatening.
This may seem inequitable and may result in claim disputes and bad publicity for the company.
Claims administration costs will rise because payments are made over an extended period and the company may wish to check from time to time that the insured is still alive.
The company will now bear a post-claim mortality and investment risk, and hence be subject to greater uncertainty.
The company will need more extensive mortality data (ie post claim) in order to price the contract accurately.
Alternatively, once claims have been agreed, the company could transfer much of the longer-term risk by buying annuities in the impaired life annuity market.
(c) Benefit levels varying with the type: This will enable the company to relate the policy benefit to the perceived seriousness of the insured's condition, ... and so should reflect the policyholder's needs at the time of claim more closely, and so is likely to appeal to customers.
A decision will need to be made on what levels of benefit would be appropriate to offer for what conditions. This may be difficult to assess.

Information that may help in this assessment includes: expected costs of medical and nursing treatment for each condition; estimates of reductions in quality of life years as a result of each condition; results of market research to assess the perceived required level of benefits for each condition; benefits offered by competitors with similar products (eg overseas).
The scale needs to be acceptable to policyholders and easy for them to appreciate and understand.
For some conditions, eg cancer, different levels may be payable at different stages of the illness, according to its severity.
This may cause difficulties for the company, eg data at this level of detail is unlikely to be available.
If the scale is set so that the amount payable for common conditions is significantly reduced, there could be substantial reductions in premiums whilst providing value for money to customers.
Rather than there being only one payment and the policy then ceasing, if a partial payment is paid the policy will continue in force until the full sum insured has been paid.
This may increase customer satisfaction and retention.
However, this will increase the number of claim payments and hence increase claim administration costs.
It may be wise to allow the scale to be reviewed as part of the policy conditions as it may become out-of-date as the result of advances in medical technology.
Selection against the office may be reduced because the benefit level for minor conditions will be less, and so there is less opportunity for 'windfall claims'.
Underwriting may need to be revised as a result of the changes. However, this could be less stringent if there is less scope for anti-selection.
(d) Direct marketing: This is where the customer is invited to apply for the product, usually directly to the insurance company. This could be by mail, advertising in newspapers or periodicals, by telephone, email or the internet.
The company will save on sales-commission payments, but will incur the costs of direct marketing, eg advertising.
Overall, there is arguably more potential for savings using direct marketing.
Products sold through direct marketing will need to be simple in order to get sufficient response from customers.
Underwriting will also need to be simple. For example, acceptance could be guaranteed to those providing satisfactory answers to a limited number of simple questions on the proposal form.
Simplified underwriting may increase the risk of selection against the office, and this should be allowed for in the pricing basis.
The use of an initial waiting period (eg six months) could also help limit anti-selection in the absence of full underwriting, but this is unlikely to be popular with consumers.
The lack of opportunity to explain features of the policy at the sales stage increases the potential for claim disputes.
The company should therefore make additional efforts to ensure the policyholder fully understands the terms and conditions of the policy.
Policy literature and sales processes should be reviewed with this in mind.

22.4.label: Question 22.4
22.4.scenario:
A health and care insurance company, based in a developed country, has a large share of the market for individual long-term income protection (IP) insurance.

However, the company's sales volumes have been falling over the last couple of years, although an initial investigation has suggested that the profit from the IP business has been increasing.

The directors of the company are concerned about the long-term prospects for the individual IP business. They are aware that the IP market is very price sensitive, and have suggested that the company's new strategy should be to use its profits to cut premiums and improve benefits on its policies.

The insurer wants to assess this suggested strategy.
22.4.question: N/A

(i).question: Describe the investigations the insurer could undertake to understand the reasons for the current situation.

Why have the sales volumes been falling?: The insurer will need to investigate whether sales have fallen because the market as a whole is shrinking or because the company's market share (as a proportion of new business sales) is falling.
As a start, it will need to compare the company's IP sales figures with those of the overall market.
There has been a trend in recent years for insurers to offer IP policies with reviewable terms rather than guaranteed ones. The analysis should consider the market for both these types of policy.

If the IP market is shrinking, the reasons for this should be investigated. For example it could be: the result of general economic factors, eg downward pressure on salary levels; the result of changed consumer preferences, eg buying more critical illness (CI) insurance instead of IP insurance in response to heavy marketing of CI policies.
External reports on the IP market and reinsurers may be good sources of information here.
If the company's market share is falling, the reasons for this must be investigated. The analysis will involve looking at trends of sales figures.
Both sales numbers and the company's percentage market share should be considered (if possible).
If data permit, monthly figures could be used as these would be more up-to-date and provide more information, ... but these should be adjusted to allow for seasonal effects, eg holidays.

The analysis should be performed for each distribution channel separately, and in a greater level of detail if possible, eg by individual financial advisers, so that further attention can be placed on the particular channels that are in decline.

For example, if particular financial advisers are selling less of the company's business, these advisers could be approached to ascertain why this is the case, eg it may be due to inefficiencies of the company's policy servicing.
If data permit, the analysis should be broken down further to identify which areas are contributing to the decline in sales, or whether it is uniform over the whole portfolio.
Further useful splits would be by: guaranteed or reviewable terms; main policy features, eg deferred period, levels of cover; main policyholder characteristics, eg ages, location, occupation.
Analysis of conversion rates, ie the proportion of applications that become sales, would also provide useful information.
Again, it could look at trends, split down by sector. For example, this could suggest delays in processing applications.
Consider pricing levels compared to those that have been offered by competitors for similar policies and compare this with the corresponding market share over the time period considered.
This could give an indication of price sensitivity and so what price levels are required to maintain market share.
If the market is price sensitive then a small price differential could have a significant effect on volumes.
Also compare benefits and terms offered by competitors to identify whether the company is in line with the market.
For example, the company's product may not have kept pace with the norms in the market, eg in allowing benefits during career breaks.
Information on competitors' strategies would be useful and may help to explain the company's declining market share.
For example, competitors may have focused on rapidly expanding niche markets, eg females in professional employment, and so increased their market shares.
Investigate the effectiveness of the company's marketing of IP products.
For example, consider whether the advertising effort is in line with competitors and whether it is appropriate for the target market.
Investigate the efficiency of the acceptance process. Have there been delays in certain stages of this process, such as underwriting?
Consider whether the remuneration levels and structures are appropriate, eg are commission terms to financial advisers in line with the market?
Investigate whether there are reasons for consumers to be deterred from taking out insurance with this particular company. For example, has it had adverse media publicity, possibly on other products?
Customer research and/or interviews with sales staff could be used to complement the above investigations in helping identify the reasons behind areas of sales decline in more detail.
Why have profits on the business increas: This could (in part) be due to increased volumes of business in force. If, despite declining sales volumes, more policies are being sold than are being lapsed, then (assuming the business is profitable) the overall profits of the IP business should increase.
In addition, if in-force volumes are increasing but sales are declining, lower per-policy overheads will contribute to increased profits.
An investigation of levels of persistency would therefore be useful.
This should be split down by the major areas, such as duration in-force, distribution channel, benefit levels, age, etc.
Alternatively, it could (in part) be due to increased profitability of the existing business.
Need to clarify what is meant by 'profits' - is this supervisory profits or an internal management figure?
An analysis of surplus should be undertaken to identify and quantify the sources of the profits.
This will show the financial effect on profit over the period (eg a year) of divergences between actual experience and that assumed in the valuation, plus the effect of new business.
For IP business, the main contributors to surplus are likely to be: actual vs expected claim inception and termination rates; actual vs expected expenses.
Other sources to consider are: changes in the valuation basis; actual vs expected investment performance; actual vs expected withdrawals; changes in the effect of tax that have not been anticipated.
For those sources that are providing the largest contribution to surplus, further analysis could be carried out to identify the reasons for the differences from expectations.
Therefore, an analysis of claim incidence and termination rate trends could help identify areas where claim experience is improving.
This could be broken down by levels such as rating factors, distribution channel and duration of claim.
Similarly, an analysis of expenses may help identify where costs have been successfully controlled or where further improvements are required.
Results of analysis of surplus from previous years would also be useful for assessing trends.
We should obtain details of any significant changes that have been made to the company or to the business it sells that might impact on profits.
For example, a one-off cost-cutting exercise would improve profits, but this may not be sustainable in the long term.
Consider and identify possible external influences on profits, eg economic conditions may affect the propensity to claim or to lapse.

(ii).question: Outline the considerations that the directors should make in assessing the strategy.

Considerations in assessing the strategy: The new strategy should ensure that the business would meet the company's requirement for profit (eg the required return on capital) and that efficient use of capital is made.

In theory, cross-subsidies should be avoided, ie the resulting prices should be accurate for each risk group, ... and so the company should only consider cutting premium rates for risk groups where profits are already being made.
However, competition and marketing constraints may restrict this.
A major factor will be the impact that the changes will have on future sales volumes. The price-sensitivity of customers will need to be carefully assessed.
The actions of competitors will heavily influence the company's future sales volumes and this will be extremely difficult to predict with accuracy.
There is therefore significant risk around the volume assumption.

The company may decide to reduce premiums or make benefit improvements in certain markets or distribution channels (eg more so where premiums are competitive), or decide only to concentrate on areas where there is less competition (eg 'niche markets') and hence not need to cut rates.
Cutting premium rates may increase the possibility of lapse and re-entry on existing business (assuming no changes are made to existing business).
The company will need to decide whether to improve benefits for its existing business as well as new business.
Improvements on both would clearly increase costs, but should lead to increased customer satisfaction and improved persistency.

Increasing benefits on new business, but not an existing business might cause existing customers to question the value of their policies ... and lead to poorer persistency and increased levels of selective lapsing.

'Added value' services, such as health information lines, may be better offered to all policies (new and existing), because the additional per-policyholder cost is small but the perceived value to existing policyholders would be significant.
Certain benefits, such as rehabilitation services, could act to improve claims experience.
Certain benefit enhancements might require additional staff training or system changes to be able to administer them.
Significant additional benefit might require changes to medical or claims underwriting.
The company should ensure that benefit enhancements are well promoted so as to achieve the desired sales. This will require increased marketing expenditure, eg sales training or marketing literature.
The company should reassess its reinsurance strategy in the light of the proposed changes.
The company should also look for alternative ways of maintaining or improving profitability, for example by reducing distribution or other expenses.
If the required profit criterion is not achievable, the company's best option may be to withdraw from the market.
However, the effect of this on the rest of the company's business, eg loss of reputation with intermediaries, should be considered very carefully, particularly as it is a major player in the market.

(iii).question: Suggest an approach to carrying out this assessment.

Assessment approach: Cutting premium rates or improving benefits will reduce per-policy profits but may increase overall volumes. An optimum level should be sought that maximises overall long-term profits.
For selected risk groups, the proposed premium rates and benefits should be profit-tested to determine whether they will generate the required levels of profit for that risk group.

In addition, the whole portfolio of business should be modelled, ie existing business together with expected new business, to assess the overall impact on long-term future cashflows and profitability of the recommended strategy.

The directors will need to decide upon the desired success criterion, eg for the profit-testing premium rates they might require a certain present value of future profits at a specified given risk discount rate.
Both models will incorporate assumptions as regards future experience under each scenario, eg claims, persistency, and allow for all the factors mentioned above (under 'Strategy').
In particular, the whole business model should also allow for the cost of capital, solvency requirements, tax regulations and reinsurance.
Sensitivity testing could be carried out to identify the relevance of the assumptions to the overall result, eg the impact of premium levels on new business volumes.
Allow for the effect that the changes may have on the mix of business and hence resulting claims or persistency experience.
Any effect on the company's other business should be allowed for, eg enhanced cross-selling opportunities might increase sales of other products.
